1.synthesis:
Jehoahaz son of Jehu reigns in Samaria for seventeen years and does evil in the sight of the Lord, following the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat who made Israel sin — a refrain that will sound through every subsequent northern king's regnal notice. The anger of the Lord is kindled against Israel and he delivers them into the hand of Hazael king of Syria and Ben-hadad son of Hazael continually. Yet Jehoahaz entreats the Lord, and the Lord hears him — for he saw the oppression of Israel, how the king of Syria oppressed them. The Lord gives Israel a deliverer — unidentified, perhaps pointing forward to Jeroboam II or to the Assyrian campaigns that eventually broke Syrian power. But Israel does not depart from the sins of the house of Jeroboam; the Asherah remains in Samaria. At Jehoahaz's death, his military establishment has been reduced to catastrophic proportions: fifty horsemen, ten chariots, ten thousand foot soldiers — what the narrator bitterly calls "for the king of Syria had destroyed them and made them like the dust at threshing."

John Gill notes that the phrase "he made Israel sin" attached to Jeroboam's name like a permanent epithet — repeated dozens of times through Kings — is not merely biographical notation but a theological verdict: the calf-worship Jeroboam established created a generational structure of sin that corrupted every king who followed. Matthew Henry observes that Jehoahaz's prayer to the Lord in the midst of ongoing sin is a revealing datum: the Lord's response does not wait for comprehensive repentance but responds to even the minimal cry of a heart partly turned toward God. Yet the deliverer God provides does not produce lasting change; Israel returns to the old pattern.

Keil and Delitzsch note that the Hazael who oppressed Israel in Jehoahaz's reign is the same Hazael whom Elisha anointed and wept over in chapter 8 — the king Elisha foresaw would "do evil to Israel." The fulfillment of prophetic word is operating even in the darkest political history.
1.historical_context: Jehoahaz's reign (ca. 814–798 BC) falls during the period of maximum Aramean (Syrian) pressure on Israel. Hazael of Damascus had already claimed much of Israelite Transjordan and severely reduced Israel's military capacity. The reduced army of fifty horsemen and ten chariots reflects a vassal state rather than an independent kingdom; Shalmaneser III's Assyrian inscriptions provide external evidence for both Israelite and Syrian military capacity in this period. The Asherah (13:6) left in Samaria — the wooden cult object associated with the Canaanite goddess Asherah — represents the persistent syncretism the northern kingdom never fully abandoned despite periodic religious reforms. The unnamed deliverer in 13:5 has been identified by various commentators as Jeroboam II, as an Assyrian king who distracted Syrian attention, or as Elisha's continued prophetic ministry.
1.literary_structure: The regnal notice follows the standard Deuteronomistic pattern: introductory synchronism (year of the parallel southern king), age/length evaluation formula, theological verdict (evil/good), narrative of defining events, death notice. Here the pattern is compressed, the narrative of oppression and prayer functioning as the defining event. The military statistics at the end (13:7) provide vivid concreteness to the theological verdict of divine judgment.
1.theological_application: The cycle demonstrated in Jehoahaz's reign — sin, judgment, minimal prayer, partial relief, return to sin — is the northern kingdom in miniature. John Gill observes that God's response to even Jehoahaz's imperfect prayer demonstrates divine compassion that exceeds covenant obligation; God hears even partial cries. Matthew Henry draws the warning: relief from judgment without genuine repentance produces not gratitude but resumed disobedience. The Asherah standing in Samaria after the deliverer arrives is the symbol of this failure: when pressure lifts, reform evaporates. For the church, the pattern warns against treating God's patience as license rather than as time given for deeper conversion.
1.christology: The unnamed "deliverer" (moshi'a, savior) given to Israel in response to Jehoahaz's prayer is a type of the greater Savior who responds to the cry of even imperfect prayer. The word moshi'a is cognate with yeshua (Jesus); each human deliverer in Kings is a shadow of the one who is Savior not merely of a season's relief but permanently. The reduction of Israel's military establishment to dust anticipates the spiritual emptiness from which only divine rescue can save — the condition of every soul apart from grace, which receives not a temporary deliverer but the eternal Son of God.

10.synthesis:
Jehoash son of Jehoahaz reigns in Samaria for sixteen years in the thirty-seventh year of Joash king of Judah. The verdict is standard: he does evil in the sight of the Lord, not departing from the sins of Jeroboam. The regnal summary is brief — only four verses — because the defining narrative of his reign comes in the Elisha deathbed scene that immediately follows and in his subsequent military success against the Arameans (narrated later in 13:14–25) and against Judah in chapter 14. He is buried in Samaria, the royal city of the north.

John Gill observes that the brevity of the Jehoash notice is structural: the narrator reserves the detailed Jehoash narrative for the Elisha pericope that follows, which dramatically raises the theological stakes of this reign above its bare regnal statistics. Matthew Henry notes that the consistent application of the Jeroboam evaluation formula to Jehoash — despite his positive interactions with Elisha and his subsequent military victories — underlines that external success and even interaction with God's prophet does not equal covenant faithfulness. The king of Israel who weeps over Elisha's death may still persist in the institutional idolatry of Jeroboam's calves.
10.historical_context: Jehoash (also called Joash) of Israel reigned ca. 798–782 BC. His reign saw a partial reversal of the Aramean pressure that had oppressed Jehoahaz, fulfilling Elisha's deathbed prophecy of three victories over Syria. He also fought and defeated Amaziah of Judah, breaking down part of the Jerusalem wall — a significant assertion of northern power. The synchronism with "Joash son of Jehoahaz" in the south (13:10) requires careful distinction between the two Joash/Jehoash figures: both north and south have kings of this name in overlapping periods, reflecting the parallel regnal chronology the narrator maintains.
10.literary_structure: This brief notice functions as a frame around the Elisha deathbed scene. The four-verse summary is not the narrative climax of Jehoash's reign; it is a biographical bracket whose content fills in through chapters 13–14. The structure of Kings frequently provides compressed regnal summaries early, then narrates the defining events at length — as with the opening of David's reign summary before the detailed narrative of his wars and failures.
10.theological_application: Keil and Delitzsch observe that Jehoash's interaction with Elisha is the most important datum of his reign theologically, yet his regnal evaluation ignores it. This teaches that moments of genuine engagement with God's word and God's prophet do not necessarily produce sustained covenant faithfulness. The church encounters this pattern in people who are genuinely moved by the Gospel but do not follow through to lasting discipleship — the seed on rocky ground of Matt 13:20–21. The evaluation of a reign is not given by its peak moments but by its sustained direction.
10.christology: The northern kingdom's continued calf-worship despite Elisha's prophetic ministry — even Jehoash's interaction with the dying prophet does not produce reform — prefigures the rejection of Christ by those who witnessed his miracles. As Jehoash received prophetic counsel and prophetic promise but did not extend them to covenant obedience, so those who encounter the greater Prophet may receive his benefits while refusing his lordship. Christ as the final Prophet, whose word does not return void (Isa 55:11), ultimately accomplishes the salvation that every human prophet's word only partially effected.

14.synthesis:
The Elisha narrative reaches its climax in one of the most poignant and theologically charged scenes in Kings. When Elisha falls ill with the illness from which he will die, Jehoash king of Israel comes down to him and weeps over him: "My father, my father! The chariots of Israel and its horsemen!" — the exact words Elisha himself used when Elijah was taken up (2:12). The king recognizes what Israel is losing: the prophetic word was the true defense of the nation, more valuable than military hardware. Elisha's final prophetic act is a dramatic object lesson: he commands the king to take arrows, place his hand on the bow with the prophet's hands over his, then shoot eastward "the arrow of the Lord's victory, the arrow of victory over Syria!" He then commands the king to strike the ground with arrows, and Jehoash strikes three times and stops. Elisha is angry: he should have struck five or six times — then he would have utterly defeated Syria, whereas now he will defeat Syria only three times.

After Elisha's death and burial, a band of Moabite raiders appears in spring. As some Israelites are burying a man, they see the raiders and throw the body into Elisha's tomb. When the body touches Elisha's bones, the dead man revives and stands on his feet. This postmortem miracle is the capstone of Elisha's ministry — even his bones carry divine power. The section closes with the narrated fulfillment of the deathbed prophecy: Jehoash defeats Ben-hadad three times — exactly the three times Elisha's angry prophecy predicted, no more.

John Gill comments extensively on the arrow ritual: the prophetic object lesson was designed to elicit faith and commitment from Jehoash, and his half-hearted response (three strikes rather than the energetic five or six that earnest prayer would have produced) reflects the mediocrity of his faith. He wanted victory but was not willing to pour himself into the faith-action that would have secured it completely. Matthew Henry draws the moral that "faint hearts obtain but faint mercies" — Jehoash's half-heartedness was met with a partial fulfillment rather than the total victory available to him.

Keil and Delitzsch observe that the revivification at Elisha's tomb is not presented as a resurrection but as a sign — the bones of a man who walked with God retain the life-giving power that characterized his ministry. The sign is directional, not comprehensive: it points forward to the resurrection of the body while not itself constituting it.
14.historical_context: Elisha's prophetic ministry spans from the time of Ahab through the reign of Jehoash — approximately fifty years of active service. His death thus closes the longest continuous prophetic ministry in the northern kingdom. The arrow ritual and the ground-striking combine two forms of prophetic symbolic action common in the ancient Near East: the prophet acts out the divine word to embody and enact it. The three victories over Ben-hadad III of Syria that the prophecy limited Jehoash to correspond with the three campaigns reported in 13:25. The postmortem miracle at Elisha's tomb (13:21) is unique in the Old Testament — the only case of a miraculous revivification by contact with a prophet's bones rather than through the prophet's direct action.
14.literary_structure: The Elisha deathbed scene is the structural climax of the Elisha cycle that began in 2:1. The repetition of the "chariots of Israel and its horsemen" formula (13:14) echoes Elisha's own cry at Elijah's departure (2:12), creating a frame around the entire Elisha narrative. The postmortem miracle is a grace note — a final sign after the prophet is gone — that prevents the narrative from closing on a note of mere biography. The section ends not with Elisha's death but with the fulfillment of his last prophecy, which is the proper termination of a prophetic career.
14.theological_application: Jehoash's three ground-strikes is the Bible's paradigmatic case of faith that does not press through to its full potential. John Gill notes that Elisha did not specify how many times; the king's three strikes and stop reflect not ignorance but spiritual tepidity. The lesson is not about a precise number but about the quality of earnestness in prayer and obedience. Matthew Henry extends this: those who pray halfheartedly, who engage with God's promises halfway, who commit to faith only until it becomes costly, should not be surprised when they receive proportional rather than complete answers. The postmortem miracle at Elisha's tomb is God's final testimony to the character of the man — his bones still carry the life that animated his ministry, because it was God's life, not merely human charisma.
14.christology: The revivification at Elisha's tomb by contact with his bones is a striking type of the resurrection life that flows from Christ. Where Elisha's dead bones impart life to another dead body, Christ risen from the tomb is himself the resurrection and the life (John 11:25), imparting life not merely through proximity to his remains but through union with his person. The miracle also underscores that the prophetic ministry is not exhausted by death: Elisha's word, embedded in the prophecy of three victories, continues to operate after his burial, just as Christ's atoning work continues to operate eternally. The deathbed scene — prophet weeping king, arrows shot, ground struck — is a picture of Christ's intercession: he goes before us, his prophetic word shapes our battles, and the degree of victory we experience corresponds to the earnestness of our faith-response.
